restatement: A single-page tic-tac-toe game where one human player plays against a computer opponent on the same device.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The application is a single self-contained page with no routing, no navigation, and no separate screens.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If multi-page was intended, navigation, routing, and shared-state plumbing would be missing.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Game uses the classic 3x3 grid with X and O markers and standard win conditions (three in a row horizontally, vertically, or diagonally) plus draw detection when the board fills.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Tic-tac-toe is a well-known canonical game; deviating from 3x3/X-O/standard rules would no longer be tic-tac-toe.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If a variant (4x4, misere, ultimate tic-tac-toe) was intended, the board renderer and win-check logic would need to change.

assumption_ledger.assumption: There is exactly one human player and one computer opponent; no online multiplayer, no local two-human mode.
assumption_ledger.rationale: The prompt explicitly says 'I play against the computer' (singular human, computer opponent).
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If two-human or online play was intended, turn ownership, input routing, and possibly networking would be missing.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The human plays as X and moves first; the computer plays as O.
assumption_ledger.rationale: X-first is the conventional default in tic-tac-toe and gives the human the familiar starting move; simplest defensible interpretation.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user expected the computer to open (or to choose), the opening flow and any first-move AI heuristic would need a tweak; cosmetic, not structural.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Players input moves by clicking/tapping an empty cell on the board.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Click-to-place is the universal interaction model for grid-based browser games; no other input method is implied.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If keyboard-only or voice input was required, an alternative input layer would be missing (still solvable, but not built).

assumption_ledger.assumption: After a game ends (win, loss, or draw), the player can start a new game without reloading the page (e.g., a 'New Game' / 'Play Again' control).
assumption_ledger.rationale: A game that can only be played once per page-load is a degenerate experience; restartability is implied by 'game'.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Without restart, the user would have to refresh manually after every match; UX would be poor but functionality would still 'work' once.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The end-state of the game is announced visibly to the user (who won, or that it's a draw) before/while offering the restart control.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Without an outcome message the player cannot tell the game is over; required for the game loop to be legible.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If outcome messaging is omitted, the player cannot tell when to restart; game feels broken.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The game runs entirely client-side with no backend, no persistence between sessions, and no user accounts.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Per the system charter, backend/auth/persistence default to absent unless required. Tic-tac-toe vs computer needs none of these.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If cross-device score history or accounts were expected, storage and auth layers would be missing.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Only the current game's state is tracked; no running win/loss/draw scoreboard across multiple games.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Simplest-within-reason: the prompt names a game, not a session of games. Scoreboard is a common nice-to-have but not implied.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user expected a tally, a small score counter would need to be added; trivially additive, not structural.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The game is played on a desktop browser by default; layout should be usable on a typical desktop viewport. Mobile responsiveness is not a stated requirement.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Per charter, mobile-first defaults to not-present unless required. Browser desktop is the simplest target for a 'single-page' web game.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: On small touchscreens, cells may be undersized or layout may overflow; visual polish issue, not a logic break.

assumption_ledger.assumption: A turn indicator (whose turn it is) is shown during play.
assumption_ledger.rationale: The computer's move happens between the human's clicks; without an indicator, very fast computer moves could feel ambiguous. Cheap to include and improves legibility.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If omitted, the player can usually still infer turn from board state; minor UX downgrade.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Once a cell is played, it cannot be overwritten or undone; the human cannot take back moves.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Standard tic-tac-toe rules; undo is not implied by the prompt.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If undo was expected, a move-history stack and revert UI would be missing.

choices.name: Unbeatable (perfect play, e.g., minimax)
choices.implication: Human can at best draw; demonstrates 'real' AI; slightly more logic to implement.

choices.name: Beatable (random or simple heuristic: take win, block win, else random)
choices.implication: Human can win sometimes; feels more like a casual game; simpler to implement.

inflection_points.default_branch: Beatable (simple heuristic: take immediate win, block immediate loss, otherwise pick a reasonable cell)
inflection_points.why_inflection: Cascade depth: shapes core gameplay feel and the AI module's complexity. UX surface: directly determines whether the player ever wins, which is the most visible aspect of the experience. Option asymmetry: a perfect minimax player and a heuristic player differ noticeably in code and in 'fun.' Reversibility: moderately reversible (AI module can be swapped) but the choice colors the whole demo.

choices.name: Minimal/utilitarian (plain grid, system fonts, basic colors)
choices.implication: Fastest to build; looks bare but unambiguous.

choices.name: Polished/themed (custom typography, animation on placement, win-line highlight, hover states)
choices.implication: More appealing demo; more styling work; subjective taste calls.

inflection_points.default_branch: Minimal but tidy: clear grid, readable X/O, hover state on empty cells, highlighted winning line on game end.
inflection_points.why_inflection: UX surface: this is the entire visible artifact of a single-page game, so style choices are highly visible. Cascade depth: low — does not fork logic. Reversibility: easy to restyle later. Option asymmetry: moderate effort difference. Worth flagging because 'good enough' and 'polished' diverge meaningfully and the prompt gives no guidance.

out_of_scope: Persistent score history across sessions or devices
Difficulty selector / multiple AI difficulty levels (one default difficulty only)
Choice of symbol or who goes first (fixed: human=X, human goes first)
Animations beyond simple state transitions (no elaborate motion design)
Variants of tic-tac-toe (4x4, ultimate, misere, gravity, etc.)
Mobile-first responsive design and touch-specific tuning
Accessibility beyond baseline semantics (no formal WCAG audit committed)
Analytics, telemetry, or any external network calls
Backend, database, or server-side logic of any kind
